4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Lessons from months of heavy GPT-5.5 Fast usage in real development work
For the past few months, I always had GPT-5.5 Fast open while working. Every thought I had went straight into it. What if we restructure this? Is this feature a good idea? Why does this part feel off?
It felt productive. I was talking to the smartest model I could afford, so I assumed I was getting leverage.
Then I looked at where my usage was actually going. Most of it was not going to hard problems. Most of it was me thinking out loud. And I was paying for it.
The mistake I kept making
The logic seemed reasonable. The model is smart. So it became my always-on companion. Product ideas, UI opinions, random what-ifs, half-baked plans. Everything went in.
But when I checked my usage, the expensive sessions were not the hard problems. The architecture decisions and production incidents were worth every token. The waste was everything else: me thinking out loud, with the meter running.
The pattern
My bad sessions all had the same shape.
I type a vague thought. The model gives a confident answer. I say “hmm, not quite.” I try again. And again.
At no point did the model tell me, “You haven’t decided what you want yet.” It can’t. It takes whatever you give it and runs with it. Fast, fluent, and completely sure of itself.
And here is the part I missed for months: the agent did not understand the problem either. How could it? I never explained it properly, because I did not understand it myself. The only difference between us was that I knew I was confused. The model just guessed, confidently.
Why this matters
Around the same time, one task went the opposite way.
Before prompting, I wrote a short brief. The problem in plain words. The constraints. What I had already decided. And where I wanted the agent to push back instead of just agreeing with me.
It came back in a few passes with work that would have taken me days. Same model, same week. The only real difference was that I did my own thinking first, before paying for the model’s thinking.
That is when the lesson clicked. AI is an amplifier. Give it clear thinking and it multiplies the clarity. Give it confusion and it multiplies the confusion, quickly and confidently.
The framework: brainstorm, then brief, then prompt
Here is the rule I follow now.
Do not hand the agent a problem you do not understand yet. It will not understand it either. It will just guess faster.
In practice, it is three steps.
1. Brainstorm until you understand the problem. Not the solution. The problem. You can even brainstorm with AI, and that is a legitimate use. But make it a deliberate session with an exit condition: “I can now say what I am actually solving.” An always-on tab has no exit condition. That is not brainstorming. That is confusion with a bill.
2. Write the brief. One sentence about the problem. What done looks like. What you have already decided. If you cannot write the brief, you are not ready to prompt. You are ready to think. Go back to step one.
3. Then prompt. Now the model is multiplying your understanding instead of amplifying your confusion.
What I do differently now
If you catch yourself doing what I did, here is what worked for me.
The tab is not always open anymore. When I open it, I know why.
Brainstorming is a decision now, not a default. I tell myself “this is a brainstorming session,” and I know what question I am trying to answer before I start.
And no brief, no prompt. If I cannot write three sentences about a task, the task is not ready for an agent. I am the blocker, not the model.
What this means for teams
AI mastery is not about knowing the perfect prompt. It is about knowing what kind of thinking you are amplifying.
If your team’s AI sessions are long, circular, and vague, the problem is usually not the model or the prompts. It is that work is reaching the AI before anyone has done the thinking. The teams getting real leverage from AI were already good at understanding problems. The AI just made them faster.
AI makes good thinking faster. It also makes bad thinking faster. The tool does not decide which one you get. You do.
At Symph, this is how we build with AI: the models move fast, and the engineers own the judgment.
